texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Recent  developments  in membrane  vapour separation systems
texts.text: Recent  developments  in membrane  vapour separation systems

texts.label: text
texts.orig: By Richard  Baker, Membrane Technology and Research Inc,  California, USA
texts.text: By Richard  Baker, Membrane Technology and Research Inc,  California, USA

texts.label: text
texts.orig: About eight  years  ago the  first membrane units  for  the  recovery  of  organic vapours from gas  streams  were installed. Since  then about 200 units  have been built. Many  of the  early  systems  were quite  small,  but more recently a number of  large  plants  have been produced to  separate  large  petrochemical  plant  gas streams.  In  this paper some recent  developments in  this  area  are  reviewed.
texts.text: About eight  years  ago the  first membrane units  for  the  recovery  of  organic vapours from gas  streams  were installed. Since  then about 200 units  have been built. Many  of the  early  systems  were quite  small,  but more recently a number of  large  plants  have been produced to  separate  large  petrochemical  plant  gas streams.  In  this paper some recent  developments in  this  area  are  reviewed.

texts.orig: The ability of  a  membrane to  separate two gases (1)  and (2)  is  characterised by the  membrane selectivity, a  defined  as  the ratio  of the permeabilities, PI  and Pc' of  the  membrane to the  gases:
texts.text: The ability of  a  membrane to  separate two gases (1)  and (2)  is  characterised by the  membrane selectivity, a  defined  as  the ratio  of the permeabilities, PI  and Pc' of  the  membrane to the  gases:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: the  relative solubilities (S/S) of  the  gases in  the membrane.
texts.text: the  relative solubilities (S/S) of  the  gases in  the membrane.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Permeability is  the  product of  the  diffusivity D, and  the  solubility S of  the gas  in  the  membrane material. Thus,  the  membrane selectivity depends on the relative diffusion coefficients (D  r D  2) of  the  two gases in  the  polymer  and on
texts.text: Permeability is  the  product of  the  diffusivity D, and  the  solubility S of  the gas  in  the  membrane material. Thus,  the  membrane selectivity depends on the relative diffusion coefficients (D  r D  2) of  the  two gases in  the  polymer  and on

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In  all polymer  materials, the diffusion coefficient decreases with increasing molecular size because  large molecules interact with  more segments  of  the  polymer  chains than  do small molecules,  favouring  the passage of small molecules such  as  methane  over  larger ones such as  propane. In  general, solubility increases with increasing condensability. For example, the solubility of  propane  and butane is greater than that  of  methane. Thus,  the  effect of  increasing permeant size  on  permeability is  a  balance
texts.text: In  all polymer  materials, the diffusion coefficient decreases with increasing molecular size because  large molecules interact with  more segments  of  the  polymer  chains than  do small molecules,  favouring  the passage of small molecules such  as  methane  over  larger ones such as  propane. In  general, solubility increases with increasing condensability. For example, the solubility of  propane  and butane is greater than that  of  methane. Thus,  the  effect of  increasing permeant size  on  permeability is  a  balance

texts.label: text
texts.orig: between the opposing effects  of diffusion coefficient, which  decreases with  increasing size, and solubility, which increases with increasing size.
texts.text: between the opposing effects  of diffusion coefficient, which  decreases with  increasing size, and solubility, which increases with increasing size.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This  balance determines the  selectivity of a membrane  for any pair  of gases and  is  a function  of the membrane  material. Glassy, rigid polymers  such  as  polysulfone  or polyimides  preferentially permeate  the  small, non-condensable gases hydrogen, nitrogen, and methane, over the larger, condensable  gases propane and  butane.  On the other  hand, rubbery  polymers such as silicone rubber (polydimethylsiloxane) preferentially permeate the larger, more  condensable gases  propane, and butane, over  the  smaller, non-condensable gases, hydrogen, nitrogen and  methane. Ill
texts.text: This  balance determines the  selectivity of a membrane  for any pair  of gases and  is  a function  of the membrane  material. Glassy, rigid polymers  such  as  polysulfone  or polyimides  preferentially permeate  the  small, non-condensable gases hydrogen, nitrogen, and methane, over the larger, condensable  gases propane and  butane.  On the other  hand, rubbery  polymers such as silicone rubber (polydimethylsiloxane) preferentially permeate the larger, more  condensable gases  propane, and butane, over  the  smaller, non-condensable gases, hydrogen, nitrogen and  methane. Ill

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For example, the  selectivity of  silicone rubber, the  most  widely  used  organic-vapour-permeable membrane material, is  shown in  Figure  1 for  a number of  vapours  found  in  the  petrochemical industry. Selectivity increases with increasing condensability of the vapour component, so butane and pentane  are  easier to  separate from nitrogen than are  ethylene and  propane.
texts.text: For example, the  selectivity of  silicone rubber, the  most  widely  used  organic-vapour-permeable membrane material, is  shown in  Figure  1 for  a number of  vapours  found  in  the  petrochemical industry. Selectivity increases with increasing condensability of the vapour component, so butane and pentane  are  easier to  separate from nitrogen than are  ethylene and  propane.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In  petrochemical and refinery operations, very good separation of  gas stream  components is often  required. Not  only must  more  than 90-95% of  the  valuable  organic vapour component be recovered  from the  gas  stream, but the  vapour must be recovered  as a pure product containing less than  5-10% of  the  other components. Simple  one-stage membrane separation units can achieve  high  recoveries or high  purity products, but  not  both  at  the  same time.  To  meet these  requirements multi-stage processes or hybrid  processes, combining  two separation methods,  must  be  used. [21
texts.text: In  petrochemical and refinery operations, very good separation of  gas stream  components is often  required. Not  only must  more  than 90-95% of  the  valuable  organic vapour component be recovered  from the  gas  stream, but the  vapour must be recovered  as a pure product containing less than  5-10% of  the  other components. Simple  one-stage membrane separation units can achieve  high  recoveries or high  purity products, but  not  both  at  the  same time.  To  meet these  requirements multi-stage processes or hybrid  processes, combining  two separation methods,  must  be  used. [21

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Two widely used  process designs are  shown in Figure  2. In  this example,  the  feed  gas to be separated contains 15%  propylene in  nitrogen. The  target  of the separation is to produce nitrogen gas  containing only  1%  propylene and a  propylene concentrate containing less than  5% nitrogen.
texts.text: Two widely used  process designs are  shown in Figure  2. In  this example,  the  feed  gas to be separated contains 15%  propylene in  nitrogen. The  target  of the separation is to produce nitrogen gas  containing only  1%  propylene and a  propylene concentrate containing less than  5% nitrogen.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The residue gas  from  this step  is then  sent to  a second membrane  unit  which  produces a nitrogen gas stream  containing less than 1% propylene. The permeate  from this second  step is  only  partially enriched in  propylene, so it is mixed  with  the  incoming feed gas.
texts.text: The residue gas  from  this step  is then  sent to  a second membrane  unit  which  produces a nitrogen gas stream  containing less than 1% propylene. The permeate  from this second  step is  only  partially enriched in  propylene, so it is mixed  with  the  incoming feed gas.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  two-step,  two-stage  system which  is shown in Figure 2a  achieves the  target separation by  linking several membrane units together,  each unit  performing a partial separation. The first unit  removes  a  portion of  the  propylene from the  feed gas  to  produce  a  concentrated permeate.
texts.text: The  two-step,  two-stage  system which  is shown in Figure 2a  achieves the  target separation by  linking several membrane units together,  each unit  performing a partial separation. The first unit  removes  a  portion of  the  propylene from the  feed gas  to  produce  a  concentrated permeate.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To  increase the  propylene  concentration in the  permeate  gas  from the  first  step,  a secondstage  membrane unit is used. The permeate  gas from this step  is  compressed  and  then passed through another unit to produce a  final permeate stream  containing 95%  propylene and a  partially  depleted residue  stream that  is recycled back  to  the  feed. By linking together the three  membrane  separation units, the target recovery for both  propylene and  nitrogen can  be achieved.
texts.text: To  increase the  propylene  concentration in the  permeate  gas  from the  first  step,  a secondstage  membrane unit is used. The permeate  gas from this step  is  compressed  and  then passed through another unit to produce a  final permeate stream  containing 95%  propylene and a  partially  depleted residue  stream that  is recycled back  to  the  feed. By linking together the three  membrane  separation units, the target recovery for both  propylene and  nitrogen can  be achieved.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A  number of multi-step vapour separation systems  of  the  type  shown  in  Figure  2a have been  installed. These  systems have the  advantage of  operating at  ambient temperatures and  having as  the  main  rotating equipment gas compressors with  which petrochemical plant  operators are very  familiar.
texts.text: A  number of multi-step vapour separation systems  of  the  type  shown  in  Figure  2a have been  installed. These  systems have the  advantage of  operating at  ambient temperatures and  having as  the  main  rotating equipment gas compressors with  which petrochemical plant  operators are very  familiar.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: However,  if  very good  removal of the nitrogen from the  recycle  gas  is required, the hybrid  process  combining  condensation  and membrane  separation shown  in  Figure  2b is preferred. E 3 1In  this design, the  compressed  feed gas is sent to  a  condenser. On cooling the  feed gas,  a portion  of the propylene  content  is removed  as a condensed liquid. The remaining, uncondensed propylene  is removed  by  the membrane  separation system to produce a 99% nitrogen  stream.  The  permeate gas  is recycled  to the incoming  feed  gas from the purge  bin.
texts.text: However,  if  very good  removal of the nitrogen from the  recycle  gas  is required, the hybrid  process  combining  condensation  and membrane  separation shown  in  Figure  2b is preferred. E 3 1In  this design, the  compressed  feed gas is sent to  a  condenser. On cooling the  feed gas,  a portion  of the propylene  content  is removed  as a condensed liquid. The remaining, uncondensed propylene  is removed  by  the membrane  separation system to produce a 99% nitrogen  stream.  The  permeate gas  is recycled  to the incoming  feed  gas from the purge  bin.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Because  the gas  sent to  the  membrane stage is cooled,  the solubility of propylene  in the membrane is enhanced, and  the selectivity of  the membrane  unit  is  increased. The  propylene condensate contains some dissolved nitrogen so the  liquid is flashed at low pressure to  remove this gas, producing  a better than  99.5% pure hydrocarbon product.
texts.text: Because  the gas  sent to  the  membrane stage is cooled,  the solubility of propylene  in the membrane is enhanced, and  the selectivity of  the membrane  unit  is  increased. The  propylene condensate contains some dissolved nitrogen so the  liquid is flashed at low pressure to  remove this gas, producing  a better than  99.5% pure hydrocarbon product.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The main disadvantage of  the  hybrid design is the  cost  of  refrigeration required to cool  and condense  the  monomer  from the  incoming  gas. For example, recovery  of propylene  requires cooling from -10°C to  -30°C.  The gas must also be  dry to avoid ice formation  in the condenser. On the  other hand,  the quality of  the separated  nitrogen  and  propylene product streams is  high.
texts.text: The main disadvantage of  the  hybrid design is the  cost  of  refrigeration required to cool  and condense  the  monomer  from the  incoming  gas. For example, recovery  of propylene  requires cooling from -10°C to  -30°C.  The gas must also be  dry to avoid ice formation  in the condenser. On the  other hand,  the quality of  the separated  nitrogen  and  propylene product streams is  high.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The first application of  vapour  recovery systems in  the  USA  was the  recovery of CFC  and  HCFC halocarbons from  industrial vent  streams such  as large refrigerators. [1]  In Europe a number  of units were  installed at  about  the  same time  to recover hydrocarbon  vapours  from air  streams produced in gasoline  loading  and  unloading operations. [4]
texts.text: The first application of  vapour  recovery systems in  the  USA  was the  recovery of CFC  and  HCFC halocarbons from  industrial vent  streams such  as large refrigerators. [1]  In Europe a number  of units were  installed at  about  the  same time  to recover hydrocarbon  vapours  from air  streams produced in gasoline  loading  and  unloading operations. [4]

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Although  the  value  of  the  recovered vapours played  a  part in  the  installation ofthese systems, these  were  primarily environmentally driven applications. More  recently, the focus  of the companies  producing this type  of equipment  has moved to  petrochemical and  refinery streams for which the  cost of  the  process can  be  justified by the  value of  the  recovered vapours. An example of this  type  of  application is  the recovery  of propylene  or ethylene  from  polyolefin resin degassing operations described here. 15]
texts.text: Although  the  value  of  the  recovered vapours played  a  part in  the  installation ofthese systems, these  were  primarily environmentally driven applications. More  recently, the focus  of the companies  producing this type  of equipment  has moved to  petrochemical and  refinery streams for which the  cost of  the  process can  be  justified by the  value of  the  recovered vapours. An example of this  type  of  application is  the recovery  of propylene  or ethylene  from  polyolefin resin degassing operations described here. 15]

texts.orig: recycled  to the resin  bin.  The  hydrocarbon liquid  stream is recycled  to the monomer purification section of  the  plant  for  upgrading prior  to recycling to the reactor. This system recovers 98%  of  the  hydrocarbon  values of  the plant.
texts.text: recycled  to the resin  bin.  The  hydrocarbon liquid  stream is recycled  to the monomer purification section of  the  plant  for  upgrading prior  to recycling to the reactor. This system recovers 98%  of  the  hydrocarbon  values of  the plant.

texts.orig: Valuing  the  olefins fraction at US$0.20/lb, this corresponds to  about  US$1 180 000.  The other  hydrocarbon  solvents and nitrogen have an  additional value of  US$200  000,  so  the  plant achieves a payback time  of  less  than one year based  on the cost of  the  skid-mounted membrane system.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The compelling economics  of  these monomer recovery  units  in polyolefin and  polyvinyl chloride plants  has led  to  30 to  40  of  these systems being installed in the last  few years. Related  applications in refinery operations are also being  explored.
texts.text: The compelling economics  of  these monomer recovery  units  in polyolefin and  polyvinyl chloride plants  has led  to  30 to  40  of  these systems being installed in the last  few years. Related  applications in refinery operations are also being  explored.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In  the  long  term, one of  the  larger applications of  the  technology may be  the  separation of  water vapour and heavy hydrocarbon  vapours from natural  gas. As  produced,  natural  gas  has a widely  varying  composition, depending  on the gas reservoir, but  for  delivery to the  pipeline the gas must  meet  rigid  specifcations. Figure  5 illustrates how  vapour separation membranes can be  used in  processing an associated natural gas  stream.
texts.text: In  the  long  term, one of  the  larger applications of  the  technology may be  the  separation of  water vapour and heavy hydrocarbon  vapours from natural  gas. As  produced,  natural  gas  has a widely  varying  composition, depending  on the gas reservoir, but  for  delivery to the  pipeline the gas must  meet  rigid  specifcations. Figure  5 illustrates how  vapour separation membranes can be  used in  processing an associated natural gas  stream.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A  simplified flow scheme  of a  polyolefin production process  is  shown  in  Figure  3.  In  a typical  polymerisation plant,  monomer and catalyst are contacted  at high pressure  in a reactor. After  polymerisation, the polymer is removed  to  a  low-pressure separator where  most of the unreacted  monomer is removed  and recycled to  the  reactor. The raw  polymer product leaving  the separator still contains  significant amounts of  sorbed  monomers  and processing solvents, which  must  be removed before  the polymer  can  be  used.  Therefore, the  polymer  is passed to  large resin  degassing  bins through which nitrogen is circulated. The nitrogen strips the  residual monomer  from the  polymer.  The vent  gas  leaving these bins  contains 10-20  vol% of valuable  hydrocarbons  in nitrogen. In the past, no economical  method of  separating these gases  was  available,  so the  stream  was flared or used  as  low-grade fuel and  the  monomer content lost. The value  of  these  potentially recoverable monomers is  high  about US$1 million annually at  a  typical polymerisation plant.
texts.text: A  simplified flow scheme  of a  polyolefin production process  is  shown  in  Figure  3.  In  a typical  polymerisation plant,  monomer and catalyst are contacted  at high pressure  in a reactor. After  polymerisation, the polymer is removed  to  a  low-pressure separator where  most of the unreacted  monomer is removed  and recycled to  the  reactor. The raw  polymer product leaving  the separator still contains  significant amounts of  sorbed  monomers  and processing solvents, which  must  be removed before  the polymer  can  be  used.  Therefore, the  polymer  is passed to  large resin  degassing  bins through which nitrogen is circulated. The nitrogen strips the  residual monomer  from the  polymer.  The vent  gas  leaving these bins  contains 10-20  vol% of valuable  hydrocarbons  in nitrogen. In the past, no economical  method of  separating these gases  was  available,  so the  stream  was flared or used  as  low-grade fuel and  the  monomer content lost. The value  of  these  potentially recoverable monomers is  high  about US$1 million annually at  a  typical polymerisation plant.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The use  of  membranes  (Figure 4) provides a solution to  this problem. In this  design  the compressed resin-bin vent gas is  sent to a condenser.  On  cooling  the gas,  most of the propylene and some of  the  ethylene are  removed as a  condensed  liquid.  The remaining uncondensed hydrocarbons  and  nitrogen  are then separated  in the membrane  unit,  which produces better than 98% nitrogen.  This nitrogen can be used  as  produced,  or  sent  to  a second membrane  clean-up  step  before  being

On  cooling  the gas,  most of the propylene and some of  the  ethylene are  removed as a  condensed  liquid.  The remaining uncondensed hydrocarbons  and  nitrogen  are then separated  in the membrane  unit,  which produces better than 98% nitrogen.  This nitrogen can be used  as  produced,  or  sent  to  a second membrane  clean-up  step  before  being

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Associated  natural gas  is the gas which is produced  together with  water  in  the  production of  oil. Large  amounts  of  gas  are  produced  from this  source.  The  low-pressure mixture  of oil, water  and gas  from the  well  is sent  to  a threephase separator. The  water (called  produced Water) is  usually re-injected into  the  ground. The oil is sent to  storage and  the  natural gas  (the associated gas) is  sent to  the  pipeline. However, this  gas,  as well  as containing methane and ethane, is  saturated with  water  and heavy  C3+ hydrocarbons, which must  both  be removed before the  gas  can  be  accepted.
texts.text: Associated  natural gas  is the gas which is produced  together with  water  in  the  production of  oil. Large  amounts  of  gas  are  produced  from this  source.  The  low-pressure mixture  of oil, water  and gas  from the  well  is sent  to  a threephase separator. The  water (called  produced Water) is  usually re-injected into  the  ground. The oil is sent to  storage and  the  natural gas  (the associated gas) is  sent to  the  pipeline. However, this  gas,  as well  as containing methane and ethane, is  saturated with  water  and heavy  C3+ hydrocarbons, which must  both  be removed before the  gas  can  be  accepted.

texts.text: simple  design and  its ability to  bring the  gas  to pipeline specifications with  no  additional pieces of  rotating equipment.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The separation of  organic vapours  from noncondensable gases using  membranes is  now  a demonstrated unit operation. The main current markets are to  treat petrochemical vent and process  streams to  recover valuable feed-stocks. However, pilot and demonstration units  have been installed in refinery and  natural gas processing  plants,  and  expansion of  the technology to  other applications in the next  few years is likely.
texts.text: The separation of  organic vapours  from noncondensable gases using  membranes is  now  a demonstrated unit operation. The main current markets are to  treat petrochemical vent and process  streams to  recover valuable feed-stocks. However, pilot and demonstration units  have been installed in refinery and  natural gas processing  plants,  and  expansion of  the technology to  other applications in the next  few years is likely.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane Technology and  Research Inc has  been  developing membrane vapour separation  technology for a  number  of years.  Key  contributors to the commercial success  of the technology  have been Doug Gottschlich, Marc Jacobs, Kaaeid Lokhandwala, Jiirgen  Kaschemekat,  Ingo Pinnau  and  Hans Wijmans.
texts.text: Membrane Technology and  Research Inc has  been  developing membrane vapour separation  technology for a  number  of years.  Key  contributors to the commercial success  of the technology  have been Doug Gottschlich, Marc Jacobs, Kaaeid Lokhandwala, Jiirgen  Kaschemekat,  Ingo Pinnau  and  Hans Wijmans.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  membrane  system shown  in Figure  5 performs the  separation. The low-pressure  gas is compressed  and cooled  to between 30°C and 35°C by  a  fan  heat-exchanger.  A portion of  the water vapour and  heavy hydrocarbons  are removed  at this point. The gas  then  passes across the  surface  of  a membrane  permeable  to the heavy hydrocarbons  and water but relatively impermeable  to  methane  and  ethane. The highpressure, dry  lean gas  which  is produced  is  sent to  the  natural gas  pipeline, and the  wet,  heavy hydrocarbon-rich permeate  is remixed  with  the low-pressure  incoming  associated gas stream. The  main advantages  of this  process are its

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A  process  using  a  hydrophobic  membrane separation process for making an  enhanced flavour  fluid, particularly a low-alcohol brew, having  improved flavour,  is  disclosed by this patent.
texts.text: A  process  using  a  hydrophobic  membrane separation process for making an  enhanced flavour  fluid, particularly a low-alcohol brew, having  improved flavour,  is  disclosed by this patent.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This patent  concerns the  isolation and purification of short  and long-chain nucleic acids from  different biological and  other starting materials. The process  is characterised by the following  steps  which  involve  the lysis  of materials containing nucleic acids; an  incubation stage  with  a mineral  supporting material; and application on membranes having  a pore  size which is  larger than the particle size  of the supporting material. Nucleic acids  are  removed from  the supporting  material  by  elution, whereby  the supporting  material  particles surprisingly remain  on  the  membrane.
texts.text: This patent  concerns the  isolation and purification of short  and long-chain nucleic acids from  different biological and  other starting materials. The process  is characterised by the following  steps  which  involve  the lysis  of materials containing nucleic acids; an  incubation stage  with  a mineral  supporting material; and application on membranes having  a pore  size which is  larger than the particle size  of the supporting material. Nucleic acids  are  removed from  the supporting  material  by  elution, whereby  the supporting  material  particles surprisingly remain  on  the  membrane.

Preferred devices combine plasma-filtration and dialysis  devices, and are  designed  to  circulate separate  sorbent

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Particulate material  including  germaniumcontaining particulate, such as germanium dioxide  (GeO2), is  captured from the  exhaust stream  loop  of  a  glass production system  by an exhaust  fan  in  a filtration system employing  a bag-house which  consists of  bag-house modules containing filter bags made from a polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) filter membrane material supported  by a PTFE  fabric backing and pre-loaded with  SiO  2  from a SiO  2  supply system.  The  captured  particulate  material s collected from  the filtration system by  an associated  collection system comprising a scrubber  fan  and scrubber  system  before the exhaust  gas  leaves the facility through the exhaust stack.  The  glass  produced by  the production system,  which consists of vapour deposition lathes, is selected such that  the concentration of  germanium in  the  particulate material collected from  the  filtration system  is at least about  2% by  weight.
texts.text: Particulate material  including  germaniumcontaining particulate, such as germanium dioxide  (GeO2), is  captured from the  exhaust stream  loop  of  a  glass production system  by an exhaust  fan  in  a filtration system employing  a bag-house which  consists of  bag-house modules containing filter bags made from a polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) filter membrane material supported  by a PTFE  fabric backing and pre-loaded with  SiO  2  from a SiO  2  supply system.  The  captured  particulate  material s collected from  the filtration system by  an associated  collection system comprising a scrubber  fan  and scrubber  system  before the exhaust  gas  leaves the facility through the exhaust stack.  The  glass  produced by  the production system,  which consists of vapour deposition lathes, is selected such that  the concentration of  germanium in  the  particulate material collected from  the  filtration system  is at least about  2% by  weight.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This  invention provides a  method and  a  system for  purifying water,  in particular chlorinated bathing water  in  a  swimming pool. It  involves circulating the  water  through a  filter device, and then  at  least  a sub-flow of  the  filtered  water, on the  clean-water side of  the  filter device, is passed through  an  ultraviolet (UV) system  for photochemical treatment. The first sub-flow is irradiated intensively  with electromagnetic radiation from one or more metal-dosed UV intermediate pressure lamps  during the  passage through  the  UV  system.  A second  sub-flow of photochemically treated water is  withdrawn from the first sub-flow  for nanofiltration r reverse  osmosis treatment using  a membrane filter device. With the  present invention it  is now  also possible  to remove  carcinogenic substances, such as  THM and AOX  from the bathing water  in  swimming pools  or the  like.
texts.text: This  invention provides a  method and  a  system for  purifying water,  in particular chlorinated bathing water  in  a  swimming pool. It  involves circulating the  water  through a  filter device, and then  at  least  a sub-flow of  the  filtered  water, on the  clean-water side of  the  filter device, is passed through  an  ultraviolet (UV) system  for photochemical treatment. The first sub-flow is irradiated intensively  with electromagnetic radiation from one or more metal-dosed UV intermediate pressure lamps  during the  passage through  the  UV  system.  A second  sub-flow of photochemically treated water is  withdrawn from the first sub-flow  for nanofiltration r reverse  osmosis treatment using  a membrane filter device. With the  present invention it  is now  also possible  to remove  carcinogenic substances, such as  THM and AOX  from the bathing water  in  swimming pools  or the  like.
